description: Thuggee was a phenomenon of highway robbery in the Indian subcontinent, where gangs of thugs murdered and robbed travelers, often by strangling. It gained prominence in the early 19th century during British colonial rule, and was portrayed as a secret fraternity of ritual stranglers. The colonial campaign against thuggee, led by William Henry Sleeman, resulted in thousands of convictions. Modern historians view thuggee as ordinary banditry rather than a religious cult.

country_links.evidence_quote: Items made by the Thugs were also exhibited at the 1862 International Exhibition in London and the 1867 Exposition Universelle in Paris.
country_links.context: Items made by the Thugs were exhibited at the 1867 Exposition Universelle in Paris.

country_links.evidence_quote: A carpet made by the Thugs was exhibited at the Great Exhibition in 1851 and they later made a 40 by 80 feet (12 m × 24 m) carpet for Queen Victoria that, as of 2005, remained on display in the Waterloo Chamber at Windsor Castle.
country_links.context: Thuggee-made carpets were exhibited at the Great Exhibition in London and a carpet was made for Queen Victoria, displayed at Windsor Castle.
